claim: Multi-agent system complexity grows exponentially—moving from 1 agent to 5 agents increases coordination problems by ~25x, not 5x, due to potential connections and failure modes
actionable: When designing multi-agent systems, account for exponential complexity scaling by implementing careful coordination patterns early, not just adding agents linearly
source_title: From Chaos to Choreography: Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns That Actually Work — Sandipan Bhaumik

claim: Immutable state with versioning (append-only, never update) eliminates race conditions and stale reads in multi-agent systems; each agent produces a new immutable state version
actionable: Implement immutable state snapshots with version numbers; agents receive a state version, process it, and append a new version without modifying the input state
source_title: From Chaos to Choreography: Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns That Actually Work — Sandipan Bhaumik

claim: Data contracts (schema validation at handoff boundaries) catch bad agent outputs immediately, preventing downstream errors from compounding three steps later
actionable: Define input/output schemas for each agent and validate contracts at every handoff; reject data that violates the contract at the boundary
source_title: From Chaos to Choreography: Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns That Actually Work — Sandipan Bhaumik

claim: Circuit breaker pattern prevents cascading failures: if an agent fails 5x in a row, open the circuit, fail fast, skip that agent, don't bombard it with requests
actionable: Wrap every agent call in a circuit breaker; track failure count, transition to half-open after timeout, test with one request before closing
source_title: From Chaos to Choreography: Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns That Actually Work — Sandipan Bhaumik

claim: Compensation (saga) pattern: every agent needs execute() and compensate() methods; on failure, orchestrator walks backward calling compensate() to roll back all changes
actionable: Design every agent operation to be reversible; implement compensate() method that undoes the work of execute()
source_title: From Chaos to Choreography: Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns That Actually Work — Sandipan Bhaumik

claim: Choreography (event-driven) vs Orchestration (centralized): choreography scales easily and decouples agents but is a nightmare to debug; orchestration is rigid but gives observability and control
actionable: Choose choreography for simple, naturally event-driven workflows with high autonomy needs; choose orchestration for complex workflows with low error tolerance (e.g., financial services)
source_title: From Chaos to Choreography: Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns That Actually Work — Sandipan Bhaumik

claim: Real-world production failure: credit decisioning system with 5 agents had 20% incorrect decisions due to stale cache; risk agent read old credit score, made wrong approval
actionable: Always invalidate cache on writes; assume your distributed system architecture will cause race conditions unless you explicitly prevent them
source_title: From Chaos to Choreography: Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns That Actually Work — Sandipan Bhaumik

claim: Choreography without strong observability will destroy you; you need bulletproof event tracing to debug which agent failed to publish or consumed twice
actionable: If using choreography, invest heavily in distributed tracing and event logging before adding agents
source_title: From Chaos to Choreography: Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns That Actually Work — Sandipan Bhaumik
